name: Responsible Users
description: When asked who is responsible, involved, assigned, working on, or blocking a request or request task, always use this UNION pattern to get ALL assigned users. The first half selects primary responsible users (pic_id), the second half selects collaborators (user_id). Only skip the second half if the question explicitly asks for the primary responsible user only. When the question mentions 'blocking progress', filter to request_tasks.status = 'in_progress' since only in-progress tasks can block.
sql: SELECT users.name FROM request_tasks JOIN users ON request_tasks.pic_id = users.id WHERE request_tasks.request_id = 50000 AND request_tasks.status = 'in_progress' UNION SELECT users.name FROM request_tasks JOIN request_task_collaborators ON request_task_collaborators.request_task_id = request_tasks.id JOIN users ON request_task_collaborators.user_id = users.id WHERE request_tasks.request_id = 50000 AND request_tasks.status = 'in_progress'

name: Task vs Request Task
description: The `tasks` table holds task definitions (name, id). The `request_tasks` table holds task instances (one per request). Use `tasks` only when the question asks about task types/names (e.g. 'tasks by frequency', 'task rollback', 'list all task names'). In that case, join through request_task_events -> request_tasks (via request_task_id) -> filter_rule_group_tasks (via filter_rule_group_task_id) -> workflow_tasks (via workflow_task_id) -> tasks (via task_id). When the question asks about task instances, collaborators, assignees, or individual task properties (e.g. 'tasks with collaborators', 'percentage of tasks'), use `request_tasks` directly — do NOT join to `tasks`.
sql: SELECT tasks.name, COUNT(*) AS event_count FROM request_task_events JOIN request_tasks ON request_task_events.request_task_id = request_tasks.id JOIN filter_rule_group_tasks ON request_tasks.filter_rule_group_task_id = filter_rule_group_tasks.id JOIN workflow_tasks ON filter_rule_group_tasks.workflow_task_id = workflow_tasks.id JOIN tasks ON workflow_tasks.task_id = tasks.id GROUP BY tasks.name ORDER BY event_count DESC LIMIT 10

name: Request Attributes
description: When a question mentions trainstop, quality level, or process by name, the actual names live in separate lookup tables, not in the requests table itself. The requests table only stores foreign key IDs (and process_ids as an array). You must JOIN to get names: trainstops via requests.trainstop_id = trainstops.id, quality_levels via requests.quality_level_id = quality_levels.id, processes via UNNEST(requests.process_ids) (since process_ids is a UUID[] array on requests).
sql: SELECT requests.id AS request_id, trainstops.name AS trainstop, quality_levels.name AS quality_level, processes.name AS process FROM requests JOIN trainstops ON requests.trainstop_id = trainstops.id JOIN quality_levels ON requests.quality_level_id = quality_levels.id JOIN processes ON processes.id = ANY(requests.process_ids) WHERE requests.status = 'in_progress'

name: Product Hierarchy
description: Pcodes, products, and business units form a hierarchy. A pcode belongs to a product (pcodes.product_id = products.id), and a product belongs to a business unit / BU (products.bu_id = bus.id). When a question asks about pcodes and business units together, always join through products. IMPORTANT: the same pcode name can exist as multiple records with different IDs, each linked to a different product and BU. When aggregating pcodes across business units, GROUP BY pcodes.name — NOT by pcodes.id. Grouping by id treats each record separately and hides cross-BU matches.
sql: SELECT pcodes.name AS pcode, COUNT(DISTINCT bus.id) AS bu_count, COUNT(DISTINCT products.id) AS product_count FROM pcodes JOIN products ON pcodes.product_id = products.id JOIN bus ON products.bu_id = bus.id GROUP BY pcodes.name ORDER BY bu_count DESC LIMIT 20

name: Percentage / Ratio Calculations with JOINs
description: When computing percentages or ratios using a LEFT JOIN, NEVER use COUNT(*) as the denominator — a LEFT JOIN can multiply rows when the right table has multiple matches per left row. Always use COUNT(DISTINCT <left_table>.id) for both numerator and denominator. Prefer the FILTER pattern: SELECT 100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) FILTER (WHERE joined.id IS NOT NULL) / COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS percentage FROM main_table t LEFT JOIN other_table joined ON ... This ensures the denominator is the true row count of the base table and the numerator only counts rows with a match.
sql: SELECT 100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT request_tasks.id) FILTER (WHERE request_task_collaborators.id IS NOT NULL) / COUNT(DISTINCT request_tasks.id) AS pct_tasks_with_collaborators FROM request_tasks LEFT JOIN request_task_collaborators ON request_task_collaborators.request_task_id = request_tasks.id
